concepts.title: Streamline Flow
concepts.conceptBody:
Streamline Flow

The streamline flow of a liquid is the flow in which each element of the liquid passing through a point travels along the same path and with the same velocity as the preceding element passing through the same point.

Hence, it is a regular flow. The path followed by each element is called streamline. The tangent drawn at any point of streamline gives the direction of the flow of liquid at that point. From figure velocity at different points may be different. Hence, in the figure

$\overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cons }\!\!~\!\!\text{ tant},\overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ constan }\!\!~\!\!\text{ t},\overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{3}}}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ constan }\!\!~\!\!\text{ t}$

but $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}\ne \overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{2}}}\ne \overrightarrow{{{\text{v}}_{3}}}$

concepts.title: Turbulent Flow
concepts.conceptBody:
Turbulent Flow 

A liquid can possess streamlined motion only when its velocity is less than a limiting velocity, called the critical velocity. When the velocity of the liquid becomes greater than the critical velocity for the liquid, the different elements of the liquid move along a zig-zag path. As a result of unsteady motion of the elements of the liquid along zig-zag paths, the liquid gets churned up. Such a motion of the liquid is called turbulent flows

concepts.title: Principle of Continuity
concepts.conceptBody:
Principle of Continuity 

 

It states that, when an incompressible and non-viscous liquid flows in a streamlined motion through a tube of non-uniform cross-section, then the product of the area of cross-section and the velocity of flow is same at every point in the tube.

Thus, ${{\text{A}}_{1}}{{\text{v}}_{1}}={{\text{A}}_{2}}{{\text{v}}_{2}}$

Or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Av}=\text{constant}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}\propto \frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}}$

concepts.title: Venturimeter
concepts.conceptBody:
Applications of principle of continuity and Bernoulli's Theorem:

(A) Venturimeter

The device is used to measure the rate of streamline flow of a fluid through a tube. The working of a venturimeter is based on Bernoulli's principle. The construction of the device is shown in the figure. The tube has different areas of cross-section ${{\text{A}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{A}}_{2}}$ in two sections. A U-tube containing mercury is fitted between the larger and narrower part of the horizontal tube. The area of cross-section of the broader part is ${{A}_{1}}$ and that of the narrower part is ${{A}_{2}}$. The flow speed of the narrower part is ${{\text{V}}_{2}}$ and that at the broader part is ${{\text{V}}_{1}}$.

The fluid is incompressible. The rate of flow of the fluid is given by,

$q={{v}_{1}}{{A}_{1}}\rho ={{v}_{2}}{{A}_{2}}\rho $ … (1)

(= the quantity (mass) of fluid flowing per second.)

Suppose that the pressure at broader end of section is ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$ and at the narrower end is ${{\text{P}}_{2}}$.

Therefore, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{P}}_{1}}-{{\text{P}}_{2}}=\text{h}{{\rho }_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}$ … (2)

where ${{\rho }_{0}}$ is the density of mercury in the U-tube.

Now applying Bernoulli's principle at the two positions, we have

${{\text{P}}_{1}}+\frac{1}{2}\rho \text{v}_{1}^{2}+0={{\text{P}}_{2}}+\frac{1}{2}\rho \text{v}_{2}^{2}+0$

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{P}_{1}}-{{P}_{2}}=\frac{1}{2}\rho \left( v_{2}^{2}-v_{1}^{2} \right)$

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\rho }_{0}}gh=\frac{1}{2}\rho \left( v_{2}^{2}-v_{1}^{2} \right)$ … (3)

Solving equations (1) and (3) for ${{\text{v}}_{1}}$, we have

${{\text{v}}_{1}}={{\text{A}}_{2}}\sqrt{\frac{2{{\rho }_{0}}\text{gh}}{\rho \left( \text{A}_{1}^{2}-\text{A}_{2}^{2} \right)}}$ … (4)

Therefore, the rate of flow of fluid is given by,

$q$ $~={{v}_{1}}{{A}_{1}}\rho $

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }q$ $~=\rho {{A}_{1}}{{A}_{2}}\sqrt{\frac{2{{\rho }_{0}}gh}{\rho \left( A_{1}^{2}-A_{2}^{2} \right)}}$
